questions.question: What distinguishes a refactoring from a regular code change?
options: A refactoring always makes the code shorter
A refactoring preserves existing behavior while improving structure
A refactoring adds new features to the codebase
A refactoring can only be done by senior engineers
questions.explanation: The defining property of a refactoring is that it is a behavior-preserving transformation — the code does exactly the same thing before and after, but its internal structure is improved.

questions.question: According to the Rule of Three, when should you refactor duplicated code?
options: As soon as you notice it the first time
When you see the second instance of duplication
When you see the third instance of duplication
Only during a dedicated refactoring sprint
questions.explanation: Fowler's Rule of Three says: the first time you do something, just do it. The second time, you wince but tolerate duplication. The third time, you refactor. This avoids premature abstraction while preventing runaway duplication.

questions.question: You have a method on class Order that repeatedly accesses fields of class Customer (name, address, discount tier). Which code smell and refactoring apply?
options: Long Method → Extract Method
Feature Envy → Move Method to Customer
Large Class → Extract Class
Speculative Generality → Inline Method
questions.explanation: A method that is more interested in another class's data than its own has Feature Envy. The fix is Move Method — move the method to the class whose data it envies (Customer), so the logic lives where the data lives.

questions.question: The same switch statement on vehicle type appears in four different functions. Which refactoring eliminates this duplication most effectively?
options: Extract Method on each switch case
Replace Conditional with Polymorphism — create subclasses for each vehicle type
Inline Method to remove the intermediate functions
Rename the switch variable for clarity
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism moves type-specific logic into subclasses. Each subclass overrides the method with its own implementation, so the switch is replaced by a single polymorphic call. Adding a new type means adding a new subclass — no existing code needs to change.

questions.question: In the safe refactoring workflow, why must you run tests after EACH small refactoring step?
options: To verify the refactoring improved performance
So that if tests break, you know exactly which small change caused it
Because refactoring always introduces bugs that must be caught immediately
To satisfy continuous integration requirements
questions.explanation: Each refactoring step should be so small that if tests break, you immediately know which change caused it. If you batch multiple refactorings between test runs, a failure could come from any of them, making it hard to pinpoint the problem.

questions.question: When should you choose to rewrite from scratch rather than refactor incrementally?
options: Whenever the code has more than 100 lines
When less than 10% of the current code will survive the changes you need and the architecture is fundamentally wrong
As soon as the code has code smells
Only when the team agrees the code is ugly
questions.explanation: Rewriting is warranted when the fundamental architecture is wrong and most code can't be preserved. However, rewrites are risky because they discard accumulated bug fixes and edge cases. If you can express the needed change as a series of small refactorings, prefer that approach.
